coredata.dc:title: A non-enzymatic, biocompatible electrochemical sensor based on N-doped graphene quantum dot-incorporated SnS2 nanosheets for in situ monitoring of hydrogen peroxide in breast cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Panda, Asit Kumar
Murugan, Keerthi
Sakthivel, Rajalakshmi
Lin, Lu-Yin
Duann, Yeh-Fang
Dhawan, Udesh
Liu, Xinke
He, Jr-Hau
Chung, Ren-Jei
coredata.dc:description: The current study reports the design and construction of enzyme-free sensor using N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)-decorated tin sulfide nanosheets (SnS2) for in situ monitoring of H2O2 secreted by human breast cancer cells. N-GQDs nanoparticles having a size of less than 1 nm were incorporated into SnS2 nanosheets to form an N-GQDs@SnS2 nanocomposite using a simple hydrothermal approach. The resulting hybrid material was an excellent electrocatalyst for the reduction of H2O2, owing to the combined properties of highly conductive N-GQDs and SnS2 nanosheets. The N-GQDs@SnS2-based sensing platform demonstrated substantial sensing ability, with a detection range of 0.0125–1128 µM and a limit of detection of 0.009 µM (S/N = 3). The sensing performance of N-GQDs@SnS2 was highly stable, selective, and reproducible. The practical application of the N-GQDs@SnS2 sensor was successfully demonstrated by quantifying H2O2 in lens cleaner, human urine, and saliva samples. Finally, the N-GQDs@SnS2 electrode was successfully applied for the real-time monitoring of H2O2 released from breast cancer cells and mouse fibroblasts. This study paves the way to designing efficient non-enzymatic electrochemical sensors for various biomolecule detection using a simple method.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of N-GQDs/SnS2 composite formation and electrochemical detection of H2O2 in human breast cancer cells.

TEM images of (a) N-GQDs, (b) SnS2, (c-e) N-GQDs@SnS2 , (f) Higher magnification TEM image of N-GQDs@SnS2, (g) SAED pattern of N-GQDs@SnS2, (h-i) TEM-EDX mapping images of N-GQDs@SnS2 nanocomposite, and (j) the corresponding EDX spectrum of N-GQDs@SnS2.
(a) XRD patterns of N-GQDs, SnS2, and N-GQDs@SnS2 and (b) Raman spectra of SnS2 , GQDs, N-GQDs, and N-GQDs@SnS2; (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of N-GQDs.
(a) XPS overall survey of N-GQDs@SnS2; high-magnified spectra of (b) Sn 3d, (c) S 2p, (d) C 1 s, (e) O 1 s, and (f) N 1 s.

(a) CV responses of bare SPE, SnS2/SPE, N-GQDs/SPE, and N-GQD@SnS2/SPE for 100 µM H2O2 in N2-saturated 0.05 M PB (pH 7.0) at a scan rate 0.05 V s−1, (b) the corresponding bar graph for reduction current versus different electrodes (c) CV response of N-GQDs@SnS2/SPE for various concentrations of H2O2 (50–350 µM), (d) linear plot of Ip c and concentration of H2O2 (µM). (e) CV response of N-GQDs@SnS2/SPE for 100 µM H2O2 at various scan rates (0.02–0.3 V s−1), (f) linear plot for the Ipc of H2O2 and scan rates (Vs−1).

(a) Amperometric (i-t) response of N-GQDs@SnS2 with successive addition of H2O2 (0.0125–1128 µM) at − 0.86 V; (b) response time for steady-state current; (c) calibration plot of Ipc versus H2O2 concentrations; (d) effects of interfering species such as ONOO-, O2, glucose (GLU) nitro benzene (NB), nitroaniline (NA), ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), and dopamine (DA) on the amperometric responses of N-GQDs@SnS2 for H2O2 in N2-saturated 0.05 M PB (pH 7.0).

Amperometric responses of N-GQDs@SnS2 to the addition of AA (6.25, 8.75, 37.5 µM) in 0.05 M PB at − 0.86 V (a) without MDA-MB-231 cells and with MDA-MB-231 cells (b) without L929 and with L929 cells. (c) Corresponding current responses for MDA-MB-231 and L929 cells with varying concentrations of AA.
Comparison of the linear range, LOD, response time, and pH of our proposed N-GQDs@SnS2 sensor with previously reported H2O2sensors.

head.ce:title: A non-enzymatic, biocompatible electrochemical sensor based on N-doped graphene quantum dot-incorporated SnS2 nanosheets for in situ monitoring of hydrogen peroxide in breast cancer cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The current study reports the design and construction of enzyme-free sensor using N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)-decorated tin sulfide nanosheets (SnS2) for in situ monitoring of H2O2 secreted by human breast cancer cells. N-GQDs nanoparticles having a size of less than 1 nm were incorporated into SnS2 nanosheets to form an N-GQDs@SnS2 nanocomposite using a simple hydrothermal approach. The resulting hybrid material was an excellent electrocatalyst for the reduction of H2O2, owing to the combined properties of highly conductive N-GQDs and SnS2 nanosheets. The N-GQDs@SnS2-based sensing platform demonstrated substantial sensing ability, with a detection range of 0.0125–1128 µM and a limit of detection of 0.009 µM (S/N = 3). The sensing performance of N-GQDs@SnS2 was highly stable, selective, and reproducible. The practical application of the N-GQDs@SnS2 sensor was successfully demonstrated by quantifying H2O2 in lens cleaner, human urine, and saliva samples. Finally, the N-GQDs@SnS2 electrode was successfully applied for the real-time monitoring of H2O2 released from breast cancer cells and mouse fibroblasts. This study paves the way to designing efficient non-enzymatic electrochemical sensors for various biomolecule detection using a simple method.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is an important reactive oxygen species (ROS) and the primary by-product of cellular metabolism. It plays a vital role in cell proliferation and apoptosis, protein metabolism, response to chronic diseases, and intracellular signal transduction [1,2]. Abnormal concentrations of H2O2 can trigger severe damage to DNA and proteins, further causing several pathological problems such as cardiovascular disorders, neurodegeneration, breast cancer, Alzheimer’s, Parkinson’s, and autoimmune diseases [3,4]. Recently, it has been reported that the concentration of H2O2 in cancer cells is higher than that in normal cells owing to the abnormal growth of tumors, which increases their H2O2 production capacity. Moreover, endogenous H2O2 is recognized as a tumor biomarker that contributes to the diagnosis of cancers [5]. Therefore, it is essential to develop a reliable and accurate method for determining cellular H2O2 concentrations under physiological conditions. Various analytical methods, including colorimetry [6], chemiluminescence [7], and fluorescence [8], have been used to monitor cellular H2O2. Nevertheless, these methods are challenging for use in in vivo and in vitro studies of H2O2 because they require tracers or unstable chemical probes [9]. Luckily, electrochemical methods are more appropriate for in situ monitoring of H2O2 owing to their high selectivity, excellent sensitivity, reproducibility, rapidity and easy process, and label-free sensing [10]. Electrochemical sensors can be classified into two main categories: enzymatic and non-enzymatic [11,12]. Enzymatic sensors have been developed based on the immobilization of peroxidase to increase the catalytic activity of the electrodes [13]. These enzyme-based electrochemical methods are well known for their high sensitivity and good selectivity [14]. However, the purification, immobilization, and stabilization of enzymes on the electrode surface are complicated, and the activity of the enzymatic electrodes easily decreases [15]. Additionally, enzyme-based methods are unstable and expensive [16]. Thus, non-enzymatic electrochemical methods with highly conductive matrices have gained significant interest because of their high catalytic activity and large surface area [17–19]. However, the development of a low detection limit of non-enzymatic H2O2 sensors remains a challenge due to the trace level of H2O2 in the cells.

Tin sulfide (SnS2) is a type of layered metal sulfide that has been extensively studied for a wide range of applications, including batteries, optoelectronics, photocatalysis, photodegradation, and electrochemical sensors, because of its cost-friendliness, non-toxicity, chemical stability, and structural similarity to graphene [20]. SnS2 is arranged in a hexagonal unit cell, where Sn is bonded to six atoms in octahedral sites [21]. SnS2 has a crystal structure similar to that of cadmium iodide (CdI2), with a longer interlayer distance (c = 0.59 nm), and the layers contain three atomic planes connected by van der Waals forces [22]. 2D SnS2 nanosheets were extensively used in electrochemical and biosensing applications owing to their high surface-to-volume ratio, higher catalytic activity, and good biocompatibility. Also, the SnS2 material can offer an encouraging microenvironment for a biological compound to carry its original structure and bioactivity and which is enhanced the electron transmission property between the electrode surface and biomolecules [23]. Nevertheless, the electrocatalytic performance of SnS2 can be further enhanced by increasing the number of active sites and boosting electrical conductivity. Various studies have proposed the incorporation of SnS2 with a highly conductive carbon matrix, including graphene, graphene aerogels, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), carbon cloth, graphitic carbon nitride, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to enhance its conductivity and produce more active sites [12,24–29]. Among various carbon materials, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) have the potential to develop heterostructures due to their excellent electronic properties and huge specific surface area [30]. By developing a 0D/2D N-GQDs/SnS2 heterostructure, the aggregation problem in the composite material can be resolved, and the number of active hetero-interfaces can be improved. There are two types of nitrogen bonding configurations within the carbon network: (i) the oxidized form (N atoms bonded to O atoms), and (ii) lattice N atoms, including pyrrolic N, pyridinic N, and graphitic N [31,32]. These N functionalities of N-GQDs are expected to improve the number of catalytic active sites and are beneficial for the detection of H2O2 at low concentrations. In this study, an enzyme-free electrochemical sensor was designed using N-GQDs and SnS2. The combination of the SnS2 nanostructure with highly conductive N-GQDs can provide an attractive platform to improve the electrochemical performance for cellular H2O2 detection in the nanomolar level.

The citric acid (C₆H₈O₇, ≥99.5 %), diethylenetriamine (DETA, HN(CH₂CH₂NH₂)₂, ≥ 99.0 %), thioacetamide (TAA, C₂H₅NS, ≥99.0 %), tin (IV) chloride pentahydrate (SnCl4·5H2O, 98 %), ascorbic acid (AA), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and all required chemicals were bought from Sigma-Aldrich, Taiwan. For the preparation of phosphate buffer (PB, 0.05 M), the mixture of sodium phosphate monobasic and dibasic (NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4 were used, pH of the PB was adjusted using the NaOH, ≥ 98 % as a base and HCl, 36.5–38 % as an acid and double distilled (DD) water was used for all washing and synthesis purposes. The screen-printed electrodes (SPEs) were acquired from Zensor (USA) (model TE-100).

The morphological characterization was investigated using a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (TEM/JEM 2100F; JEOL Ltd., Japan) and a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM/JEOL JSM- 7100F). The elemental percentages and compositions were observed using energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The crystallinity and structural defects in carbon materials were detected via X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, X′Pert3 Powder, PANalytical, Netherlands) and Raman spectroscopy (Ramboss 500i Micro (DINGWOO/USA). Electronic transitions were examined via ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy (SP-8001/Yoyu). The electrochemical studies were carried out in CH Instruments 6041E workstation (Shanghai Chenhua, China). In cyclic voltammetry (CV) analysis, a single SPE was used. Rotating disc electrodes (RDE) with a rotating speed of 1200 rpm, Pt wire, and Ag/AgCl are act as auxiliary, and reference electrodes for the amperometry studies and throughout the amperometry experiments were done at a potential of − 0.86 V. The geometric surface area of the RDE electrode used for the amperometric experiments is 0.21 cm2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N-GQDs were obtained under hydrothermal conditions according to a previously reported procedure [33] The citric acid (1 mM) and DETA (3 mM) were added to DD water (50 mL) and stirred in the solution until form a uniform mixture. Subsequently, the mixture was subjected to hydrothermal reaction at 180 ○C for 6 h. Once the autoclave reached room temperature (RT), the product was washed by centrifugation using ethanol at 6000 rpm. The material was fully dried at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain N-GQDs. GQDs was synthesized under the above procedure without addition of DETA.

The hydrothermal method was used to synthesize the N-GQDs@SnS2 composite ( Fig. 1). First, SnCl4.5H2O (1 mM) and TAA (2 mM) were added to DD water (50 mL); then, 10 mL of N-GQDs (12 mg mL−1) was mixed with this solution, followed by 30 min of stirring. Subsequently, the mixture was subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 160 °C for 12 h. The hydrolysis or dissociation reactions of TAA in the aqueous solution would create sulfur ions and proceed quicker at high temperatures. The released S2− will combine with Sn4+ in the reacted solution, which produced the SnS2 nanosheets. Then, the product was collected by centrifugation, washed multiple times with DD water and ethanol. The final product was obtained after drying and denoted as N-GQDs@SnS2. The same procedure was adopted to produce pristine SnS2 without the addition of N-GQDs. The simple sonochemical method was used to exfoliate the SnS2 sheets.

1 mg mL−1 of synthesized N-GQDs@SnS2 nanoparticles was dispersed in DD water using ultrasonication for 30 min to get consistent slurry. 6.0 µL of N-GQDs@SnS2 suspension was drop cast on the surface of SPE with the help of micropipette and exposed to dry at 50 °C for 30 min to acquire N-GQDs@SnS2/SPE. Bare SPE, N-GQDs/SPE, and SnS2/SPE were prepared as control electrodes [34].

Mouse fibroblasts (L929) and Triple-negative breast cancer cells (MDA-MB-231) were cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) supplied with antibiotics penicillin (100 U mL−1), streptomycin (100 µg mL−1), FBS (10%), and C6 cells were seeded using T75 flasks in a CO2 incubator kept at 37 °C, and 95 % of humidity.

Biocompatibility of the N-GQDs@SnS2was analyzed by MTT assay. First, the MTT (5 mg mL−1) reagent was prepared and kept at 4 °C. To assess the in vitro cytotoxicity of N-GQDs@SnS2, the stock solution was diluted 10 times with the DMEM culture medium. L929 and MDA-MB-231 cells (10000 per well) were cultured using 96-well plates for 24 h. After that, the cells were washed twice using PBS. Following, 0.5 mL of MTT reagent in DMEM medium was supplied to the cells and the plates were incubated in the dark for 4 h. Then, the MTT reagent was eliminated, and formazan crystals were dissolved by using DMSO (200 µL/well). After that, the plates were kept for slight shaking for 10 min at RT. Finally, the absorbance was determined via a microplate reader at 570 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of H2O2 in live cells
ce:section.ce:para: L929 and MDA-MB-231 cells were cultured in a DMEM medium for confluence. The medium was removed, once the cells grown-up to 90 % confluence. Afterward, the cells were washed thrice using PB and collected by centrifugation. The L929 and MDA-MB-231 cells were counted by a hemocytometer. 2 × 107 of cells were dispersed in PB (80 mL, pH 7.0) then, AA was used to stimulate H2O2 production. Amperometry experiments were conducted in cells at a potential of − 0.86 V. The PB with AA mixture without cells was used as the blank.

The morphological features of N-GQDs, SnS2, and N-GQDs@SnS2 were studied using TEM, and the corresponding results are presented in Fig. 2. The higher magnified TEM image of N-GQDs in Fig. 2a shows that the N-GQDs are nanodots. The TEM observation of pristine SnS2 shown in Fig. 2b, confirms the formation of SnS2 nanosheets with highly transparent structures. Moreover, the lattice spacings of 0.28 nm and 0.32 nm corresponding to the (101) and (100) crystal planes are indicating a high crystallinity of SnS2 nanosheets. TEM images of N-GQDs@SnS2 are displayed in Fig. 2c-e. N-GQDs were effectively anchored to the surface of SnS2. Fig. 2f shows a higher magnification of GQDs@SnS2, which exhibits obvious interplanar spacing of 0.59 nm, corresponding to the (001) plane of SnS2. However, it can be noted that the TEM image of the N-GQDs@SnS2 indicates the interlayer spacing of SnS2 is larger than that of pristine SnS2 due to the intercalation of the N-GQDs. Moreover, the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of N-GQDs@SnS2 displays obvious rings, which can be indexed to (100), (101), (110), and (202) crystal planes for SnS2 (Fig. 2g). Additionally, EDX mapping of N-GQDs@SnS2 (Fig. 2h and i) confirmed that tin (Sn), sulfur (S), carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) were evenly distributed in the composite. Sn, S, C, N, and O were detected in the EDX spectrum of N-GQDs@SnS2, with weight percentages are 26.8, 1.52, 47.48, 12.03, and 12.18 %, respectively, further confirming the formation of the nanocomposite (Fig. 2j).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to examine the crystalline structures of SnS2, N-GQDs, and N-GQDs@SnS2 ( Fig. 3a). As shown in Fig. 3a, a typical peak for the crystalline graphite-like structure of N-GQDs was observed at a 2θ value of 26.94° corresponding to the (002) plane (JCPDS Card No.: 41–1487) [35]. The diffraction peaks of pristine SnS2 at 2θ angles of 15.07°, 28.38°, 32.22°, 42.05°, 50.09°, 52.5°, 55.11°, 58.45°, 60.80°, 67.38°, and 70.53° relate to (001), (100), (101), (102), (110), (111), (103), (200), (201), (202), and (113) planes of hexagonal SnS2 phase (JCPDS 23–0677) [36], respectively. The XRD pattern of N-GQDs@SnS2 exhibited the characteristic peaks of both SnS2 and N-GQDs with high crystallinity, indicating the successful incorporation of N-GQDs into the interlayer spacing between the SnS2 nanosheets. Moreover, broadening in the diffraction peak of SnS2 at 15.07° corresponding to the (001) plane reveals the polycrystalline nature of SnS2 during composite formation [37].

Structural defects of the prepared materials were inspected via Raman spectroscopy. Raman spectrum for SnS2, GQDs, N-GQDs, and N-GQDs@SnS2 were given in Fig. 3b. Two intensive characteristic peaks of the carbon materials, D (1349 cm−1) and G (1565 cm−1) bands, were observed for GQDs, N-GQDs, and N-GQDs@SnS2. The D band indicates disorders in sp2 hybridized carbon, and G refers to in-plane vibrations of sp2 carbon (CC) [35]. In N-GQDs, the intensity ratio of D and G bands (ID/IG) was higher (∼1.00) than that of the GQDs (∼0.94), signifying that doping of N atoms into the carbon network leads to a disordered structure. The ID/IG of N-GQDs@SnS2 (1.01) was similar to that of N-GQDs, indicating the absence of structural disorders in N-GQDs during composite formation. In addition, pristine SnS2 showed two peaks at ∼313 and ∼198 cm−1, indicating that the A1 g and Eg modes of SnS2 are significant evidence for SnS2 formation [38]. Compared to those for the pristine SnS2, the peaks for A1 g and Eg modes almost diminished in the N-GQDs@SnS2 composite due to the strong interaction of SnS2 and N-GQDs. Moreover, the disappearance of SnS2 characteristic peaks in the composite owing to the presence of D and G bands with high intensity.

UV–vis absorption spectra was verified for N-GQDs (Fig. 3c). The absorption peak for the π − π * electronic transition was observed at 336 nm. In addition, another absorption edge around ∼ 350 − 400 nm was detected for N-GQDs owing to the n − π * transition of the carboxyl group. An additional peak at ∼ 450 − 500 nm was also observed owing to the n − π * transition of the C−N bond [39]. These results confirmed the formation of N-GQDs, and strong absorption peaks of the C-N electron transition provided evidence for the presence of N atoms in the GQDs network.

XPS was used to study the bonding, chemical states, and composition of the N-GQDs@SnS2 composite. The overall survey of the N-GQDs@SnS2 composite indicated the presence of individual elements such as Sn, S, C, O, and N ( Fig. 4a). In the survey spectrum, intensive peaks at around 1000 and 1090 eV indicate the sequence of peaks labeled O KLL and Sn MNN, respectively [40,41]. Two peaks were observed in the spectrum of Sn 3d (Fig. 4b) at 495.4 and 487.0 eV for Sn 3d3/2 and Sn 3d5/2, respectively, with an energy difference of 8.4 eV, indicating the presence of Sn4+ instead of Sn2+ [42]. The S 2p spectrum had two peaks at 168.3 and 162.9 eV owing to the presence of S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 respectively, representing the presence of S2- in SnS2 [43] (Fig. 4c). The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 4d) displayed three peaks at 288.8, 287.5, and 284.8 eV arising from the OC-O, C-N, and CC/C-C bonds, [44] respectively. Three peaks were found in the O 1s spectrum at 533.9, 532.2, and 530.9 eV belonging to the C-OH, C-O-N/C-O, and C=O bonds, respectively (Fig. 4e)[45]. The three peaks were fitted to the N 1 s spectrum at 402.68, 400.02, and 399.5 eV, which agreed with the graphitic-N, pyrrolic-N, and pyridine-N [46] (Fig. 4f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of H2O2 at N-GQDs@SnS2/SPE
ce:section.ce:para: The electrical resistance of the bare SPE and modified electrodes such as SnS2/SPE, N-GQDs/SPE, and N-GQDs@SnS2/SPE was studied using EIS in a mixture of the solution containing 0.1 M KCl, 5 mM K3[Fe (CN)6], and K4[Fe (CN)6]. Fig. S1 shows the obtained Nyquist plots of all the electrodes, which were fitted using the Randles equivalent circuit model (inset of Fig. S1). The charge transfer resistance (Rct) was obtained from the representative semicircle in the high-frequency region and a straight line in the low-frequency region specifies Warburg diffusion (Zw). The N-GQDs/SPE has a lower Rct value (117.5 Ω) than that of bare SPE (322.9 Ω), and SnS2/SPE (161.8 Ω), due to the high conductive N-doped GQDs. Remarkably, the resistance of N-GQDs@SnS2/SPE (74.5 Ω) is much lesser than that of the other electrodes. EIS analysis demonstrated that the N-GQDs@SnS2 material with good conductivity is anticipated to enhance the catalytic activity toward H2O2 detection.

The electrocatalytic activity of bare SPE, SnS2/SPE, N-GQDs/SPE, and N-GQDs@SnS2/SPE for reduction of 100 µM H2O2 was estimated via CV in N2-saturated 0.05 M PB (pH 7.0) at a scan rate of 0.05 V s−1 ( Fig. 5a). Bare SPE did not show an obvious peak for H2O2, demonstrating the poor catalytic active surface. Alternatively, the modified SPEs with N-GQDs and SnS2 enhanced the peak intensities owing to the reduction of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to water (H2O) with 2e- transfer on the electrode surface. The corresponding reduction peak currents (Ipc) of SnS2/SPE and N-GQDs/SPE were − 7.4 and − 10.8 µA, respectively. Compared to other electrodes, N-GQDs@SnS2/SPE exhibited a much higher current response (12.7 µA) owing to its excellent conductivity and the synergistic effect of SnS2 and N-GQDs. Fig. 5b shows the bar diagram for the obtained current response of 100 µM H2O2 and different modified electrodes. The CV response of N-GQDs@SnS2 was recorded before and after the addition of H2O2 is presented in Fig. S2. It can be seen that there was no reduction peak was observed in the N2-saturated 0.05 M PB. After the addition of 100 µM of H2O2, a well-defined reduction peak of H2O2 was observed, indicating that the N-GQDs@SnS2 composite effectively catalyzed the reduction of H2O2 to H2O. The excellent catalytic activity due to N doping of GQDs could induce an effective active site, also low resistance of the N-GQDs@SnS2 composite could induce a fast electron transfer property. The electrochemical sensitivity of N-GQDs@SnS2/SPE was studied with varying concentrations of H2O2. Fig. 5c presents the CVs of H2O2 at various concentrations (50–350 µM) on N-GQDs@SnS2/SPE in PB (pH 7.0). When the concentration of H2O2 increased from 50 to 350 µM, the current intensity was increased linearly. The calibration plot of Ipc against H2O2 concentration is shown in Fig. 5d. The regression and coefficient are as follows: Ipc (µA) = −0.0254 [H2O2] (µM) − 1.701 (µA) and R2 = 0.9879, respectively.

The electrokinetic characteristics of N-GQDs@SnS2/SPE towards 100 µM H2O2 were examined via CV at increasing scan rates (0.02–0.3 Vs−1) (Fig. 5 e). When scan rates increased from 0.02 to 0.3 V s−1, Ipc of H2O2 also increased and shifting happens in reduction peak potential (Epc) towards the negative side. Linearity was observed for the Ipc of H2O2 against scan rates; the regression and coefficient were as follows: Ipc (µA) = −63.922 (V s−1) – 5.671 and R2 = 0.99, respectively (Fig. 5 f). The values indicated that the electrochemical reduction of H2O2 on N-GQDs@SnS2/SPE is a surface-controlled process. A straight line is plotted against the log of Ipc vs. log of scan rate with a slope of 0.519 (Fig. S3a), which is confirmed that the H2O2 reduction at N-GQDs@SnS2/SPE was an entirely surface-controlled process. The plot of current versus square root of scan rates (Fig. S3b) obeys the following relationship I pc (μA) = – 45.638 υ1/2 (Vs−1)1/2 + 1.513 (R2 = 0.974). This equation revealed that the electrochemical process at GQDs@SnS2/SPE is not mixed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Amperometric determination of H2O2 at N-GQDs@SnS2
ce:section.ce:para: Optimization of appropriately applied potential is most important for amperometric H2O2 detection. The amperometric current vs time (i-t) plot was obtained at various potentials (−0.84, −0.85, −0.86, and −0.87 V) with the consecutive addition of H2O2 (7.5–13.75 µM). The most stable and highest Ipc with low background noise was observed at − 0.86 V than other potentials (Fig. S4a). A linear curve was noted between Ipc and [H2O2] over various potentials (Fig. S4b). Where a potential of − 0.86 V was found suitable for amperometry-based H2O2 detection.

Once the optimum potential was selected, the performance of N-GQDs@SnS2 in sensing H2O2 was investigated. Fig. 6a shows the i-t response of N-GQDs@SnS2 with the consecutive injection of H2O2 at 50 s intervals under continuous stirring. As represented in Fig. 6a, Ipc increased rapidly with the addition of H2O2, and a steady-state current was attained within 3 s of each addition (Fig. 6b), indicating the rapid adsorption and electrochemical reduction of H2O2 on the N-GQDs@SnS2 electrode. The obtained amperometric current of H2O2 versus concentration was plotted in Fig. 6c. A linear relationship was observed for N-GQDs@SnS2 in the range of 0.0125–1128 µM with linear regression, and the coefficient was expressed as Ipc (µA) = −0.0414 [H2O2] (µM) + 61.87 and (R2) = 0.999. Based on the IUPAC equation LOD and LOQ were calculated. LOD = 3 S/k, and LOQ = 10 S/k respectively, where ‘k′ specifies the slope value (0.0329 µA µM−1) attained from the calibration plot and ‘S′ designates the standard deviation from three blank currents (0.0001 µA), the LOD and LOQ were found to be 0.009 µM and 0.030 µM respectively. The linear range, LOD, and response time of the N-GQDs@SnS2 sensor were compared with the literature ( Table 1). Notably, the sensing parameters of the N-GQDs@SnS2 platform were much superior to that of the H2O2 sensors in the literature and could overcome the limitations of enzymatic sensors.

The selectivity of N-GQDs@SnS2 towards the reduction of H2O2 was studied using various interferents. The i-t observation for H2O2 with the presence of some potential interferences at N-GQDs@SnS2 were studied in N2-saturated PB (pH 7.0) at the optimized applied potential (Fig. 6d). Hundred-fold excess concentrations of ONOO-, O2, glucose (GLU) nitro benzene (NB), nitroaniline (NA), ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), and dopamine (DA) had no significant effects over the H2O2 reduction current response; contrastingly, H2O2 addition significantly enhanced the response. The results indicated that N-GQDs@SnS2 has excellent selectivity for the determination of H2O2 and is highly resistant to any errors caused by interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability, repeatability, and reproducibility of the N-GQDs@SnS2 sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The stability of N-GQDs@SnS2/SPE during the detection of 100 µM H2O2 was examined by repetitively scanning it (50 scans) at a scan rate of 0.05 V s−1 (Fig. S5a). After 50 cycles, the current response of N-GQDs@SnS2/SPE toward H2O2 was retained at 90 % from its initial (1st cycle) response, indicating remarkable electrochemical stability of the N-GQDs@SnS2/SPE electrode. The long-term stability test for our proposed N-GQDs@SnS2/SPE was performed in CV over 15 days using 100 µM H2O2 and obtained result was presented as a bar diagram in Fig. S5b. The current response was recorded every day using the same N-GQDs@SnS2/SPE and the electrode was kept in a 4 °C refrigerator. The N-GQDs@SnS2/SPE is retained 93.0 % of its initial day. From this result, it was proved that the N-GQDs@SnS2/SPE has excellent stability.

The repeatability and reproducibility of the N-GQDs@SnS2/SPE were studied for 100 μM H2O2 via CV. For the repeatability test, five independent CV measurements were carried out for the detection of H2O2 (Fig. S6a). Number of measurements and the Ipc were plotted, and the obtained relative standard deviation (RSD) was 3.34 % (Fig. S6b). A sequence of five different N-GQDs@SnS2 modified electrodes was used for the reproducibility test and a CV experiment was conducted for 100 µM H2O2 (Fig. S6c). As shown in the bar diagram (Fig. S6d), five different electrodes versus Ipc exhibited minor deviation in the Ipc with RSD = 3.80 % could highlight the exceptional reproducibility of the N-GQDs@SnS2 sensor.

The practical application of the N-GQDs@SnS2 sensor was assessed via amperometric (i-t) measurement of H2O2 in contact lens cleaner, human urine, and saliva samples, the lens cleaner was purchased from Watson, Taiwan. First, amperometry measurement was done by directly adding the untreated real samples and it was found that there was no H2O2. Then, the lens cleaner was 10 times diluted using PB, and H2O2 with known concentration was injected into the stock solution. The amperometry H2O2 response was observed at − 0.86 V for the addition of a lens cleaner sample containing H2O2 (2–15 µM) in N2-saturated PB (Fig. S7a). Likewise, urine and saliva samples were diluted separately with buffer and filtered using Whatman filter paper. Subsequently, a particular concentration of H2O2 was added to these samples and obtained amperometry results were displayed in Fig. S7c and S7e, respectively. The calibration plots of Ipc versus [H2O2] for lens cleaner, urine, and saliva samples are shown in Fig S7b, S7d, and S7f. The recoveries of lens cleaner, human urine, and human saliva samples are 94.8 %, 98.6 %, and 104.5 % respectively, given in Table S1. A real sample study revealed that the N-GQDs@SnS2 platform could be exploited as an excellent tool for the detection of H2O2 from substances present in the physical environment.

The biocompatibility of N-GQDs@SnS2 with the L929 and MDA-MB-231 cells was investigated before the H2O2 cell detection. L929 and MDA-MB-231 cells were cultured with N-GQDs@SnS2 composite with various concentrations (6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100 µg mL−1), and the MTT assay was performed to study cell viability. Cell viability decreased with the increasing NPs concentration. More than 90 % of viability was observed for L929 and MDA-MB-231 cells at 100 µg mL−1 (Fig. S8a), indicating excellent biocompatibility of the N-GQDs@SnS2. Hence, N-GQDs@SnS2 could be suitable materials for biological analysis. The microscopic images of L929 and MDA-MB-231 cells cultured with the N-GQDs@SnS2 nanocomposite were shown in Fig. S8b and S8c. The cells seemed to proliferate and healthy after 24 h of incubation with N-GQDs@SnS2 (10 µg mL−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of H2O2 released by live cells
ce:section.ce:para: The amperometric experiment was carried out to determine H2O2 released from the MDA-MB-231 and L929 cells. AA was used to stimulate H2O2 release from live cells. MDA-MB-231 and L929 cells were cultured and the current was measured by amperometry measurement at a potential of − 0.86 V. The same experiment without MDA-MB-231 and L929 cells were also performed as blank. No current response was observed after the addition of AA to the PB in the absence of cells. Moreover, the H2O2 peak current increased with the increase of AA concentration (6.25, 8.75, and 37.5 µM) into PB containing MDA-MB-231 and L929 cells ( Figs. 7a and 7b). The obtained current response of H2O2 released from live cells is plotted in Fig. 7c. These results suggest that N-GQDs@SnS2 could be a favorable catalyst for detecting H2O2 levels in live cells.

Herein, we reported a simple and unique approach to fabricate N-doped graphene quantum dots-decorated ultrathin SnS2 nanosheets (N-GQDs@SnS2) using a simple hydrothermal method. The TEM, FESEM, EDX, XRD, Raman, UV–vis, and XPS observations confirmed the effective formation of the N-GQDs@SnS2 composite. N-GQDs@SnS2 modified with SPE was exploited as a potential enzyme-free electrochemical sensor for the determination of H2O2. Moreover, the N-GQDs@SnS2 sensor displayed superior analytical parameters, such as a linear detection range (0.0125–1128 µM) and LOD (0.009 µM at S/N = 3). The selectivity, stability, and reproducibility of the N-GQDs@SnS2 sensor was successfully demonstrated. The practical utility of the N-GQDs@SnS2 sensor has demonstrated the quantification of H2O2 in real samples, namely lens cleaner, human urine, and saliva samples with the recoveries of 94.8 %, 98.6 %, and 104.5 % respectively. Lastly, we established that the N-GQDs@SnS2 could be successfully done for the detection of H2O2 in MDA-MB-231 and L929 cells. These results showed that levels of H2O2 release in cancer cells are higher than in normal cells.
